ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Dragon Triple Rice; A00LC (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible category-level code.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Dragon Triple Rice served at a Singapore Indian mixed-rice stall typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a mound of steamed rice topped with a portion of stir-fried mixed vegetables, a fried or scrambled egg, and a serving of cooked chicken.

nutrient_source.title: Dragon Triple Rice (Veg/Egg/Chicken) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Dragon Triple Rice (Veg/Egg/Chicken), cr_id G00010754, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB entry and is set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (boiled)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dragon Triple Rice. GI estimated at 65 based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for boiled white rice (GI ~64–72; median 65 used). The dish is predominantly white rice (~70% by weight), with mixed vegetables, egg, and chicken moderating the glycaemic response slightly. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 25.19 × 3.50) / 100 = 57.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; borderline for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced salt and soy sauce during preparation; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (327 mg K : 231 mg Na per 100 g) and dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100 g partially offset sodium impact, but portion control remains essential to keep total sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (25.2 g/100 g) with medium GI (~65); fibre and protein moderate glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to control total carbohydrate load; the dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100 g and protein at 6.5 g/100 g help attenuate postprandial glucose rise, and choosing brown or mixed-grain rice variants when available would further lower the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (4.21 g/100 g), negligible sugar (0.21 g/100 g), and meaningful dietary fibre (3.7 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low total fat content, absence of reported trans fat, and high dietary fibre make this dish broadly suitable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease; however, sodium at 231 mg/100 g warrants attention and patients should request minimal added oil and salt during preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory micronutrients from vegetables and egg; absence of trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The vegetable and egg components contribute antioxidants and anti-inflammatory micronutrients supportive of immune modulation; nightshade-sensitive RA patients should confirm the vegetable mix does not include chilli or bell pepper, and minimal oil should be requested to limit saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dragon Triple Rice (Vegetable, Egg and Chicken) is a popular Singaporean Indian mixed-rice dish sold at hawker centres and food courts, particularly at stalls operating under the 'Dragon' brand. The dish consists of a generous serving of steamed white rice accompanied by three toppings: stir-fried mixed vegetables (commonly including cabbage, carrots, and leafy greens), a cooked egg (typically fried or scrambled), and a portion of seasoned chicken (commonly braised, fried, or curried). The combination delivers a balanced macronutrient profile per 100 g: 164 kcal energy, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with a notable dietary fibre content of 3.7 g and a meaningful iron contribution of 2.55 mg. Sodium is 231 mg/100 g, placing it in the borderline range for hypertension management, while potassium at 327 mg/100 g provides a favourable offset. The dish has a medium estimated glycaemic index of approximately 65, driven primarily by the white rice base, with the protein, fat, and fibre from the toppings moderating the postprandial glucose response. Clinically, it is broadly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (with portion control), hypertension (with reduced-salt preparation), and cardiovascular disease (due to low fat and meaningful fibre). It is a halal-friendly, dairy-free, and egg-containing dish that is a staple lunch and dinner option across Singapore's multicultural food landscape.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010754) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled white rice as the closest analogous food, which is well-supported and documented.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Dragon Triple Rice is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010754) as of 2026-04-16. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this is accepted as a confirmed zero per the HPB database entry, noting that the egg component may contribute trace cholesterol that falls below the reporting threshold used by HPB. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for Dragon Triple Rice was found in any approved source. GL per 350 g serving = (65 × 88.2 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 57.3. 5. The dish is classified as Indian cuisine based on the input record's cultural metadata and its association with Indian mixed-rice stall culture in Singapore. 6. Halal status is inferred from the Indian Muslim hawker context; formal halal certification should be verified at point of purchase. 7. Aliases consistency self-check performed: all strings present in aliases_by_language (english, chinese, malay, tamil, hindi, romanized) verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. The entry 'Dragon Nasi Tiga Rasa' (malay aliases_by_language) and 'Nasi Tiga Rasa Dragon' (romanized aliases_by_language) are both present in the flat aliases array.
